content.text: What is Hybrid Cloud?

A hybrid cloud combines a private cloud (or on-premises infrastructure) with one or more public clouds, connected by technology that allows data and applications to move between them. The defining characteristic is not where resources live but that workloads can span both environments under a unified management and identity plane.

In the Azure context, hybrid cloud almost always means on-premises or private infrastructure connected to Azure, managed through Azure Arc. The connectivity may be an ExpressRoute circuit, a Site-to-Site VPN, or simply HTTPS-based Arc agent polling — depending on whether the data plane or just the control plane needs to traverse the link.
Key insight:

Hybrid is not a midpoint between private and public — it is a permanent operating model for organizations that need both. Regulators may require sensitive data to stay on-premises while burst capacity, global CDN, and SaaS tooling live in Azure.

attrs.alt: Azure Hybrid Cloud Architecture showing on-premises resources, Azure Arc management plane, and Azure public cloud connected via ExpressRoute or VPN
attrs.title: Azure Hybrid Cloud Architecture

content.text: Why Organizations Choose Hybrid Cloud

No single cloud model satisfies every constraint. Hybrid cloud exists because real enterprises operate under competing pressures that a pure public or pure private model cannot simultaneously satisfy.
Driver
What It Requires
Hybrid Solution
Data residency / sovereignty
PII or medical data cannot leave the country
Store data on-prem; process analytics in Azure
Existing capital investment
5-year server lease, significant depreciation schedule
Run steady-state on-prem; burst new workloads to Azure
Cloud migration pace
Cannot lift-and-shift everything at once
Move workloads incrementally over 2–5 years
Latency sensitivity
Manufacturing SCADA systems need < 1 ms response
Edge compute on-prem; telemetry/ML in Azure
Regulatory air-gap + cloud tools
Defense workloads but need modern DevOps
Azure Government + Arc for classified on-prem
Seasonal demand peaks
E-commerce 10x traffic in November–December
Base capacity on-prem; burst to Azure VMSS
Azure Hybrid Cloud Technologies
Microsoft provides a stack of services specifically designed for hybrid scenarios. They fall into four categories: connectivity, management, identity, and data/storage.
Azure Arc: The Hybrid Management Plane

Azure Arc projects on-premises and multi-cloud resources into Azure Resource Manager (ARM). Once a server, Kubernetes cluster, or SQL instance is Arc-registered, it appears in the Azure portal and accepts Azure Policy, RBAC, Defender for Cloud, and Update Manager — the same tools used for native Azure resources.

# Arc-enable a Linux server (run on the target machine)
curl -L https://aka.ms/azcmagent-linux | bash
azcmagent connect \
  --tenant-id "<tenant>" \
  --subscription-id "<sub>" \
  --resource-group "hybrid-rg" \
  --location "eastus" \
  --cloud "AzureCloud"

# Verify: the server now appears in Azure Portal -> Azure Arc -> Servers
az connectedmachine show \
  --resource-group hybrid-rg \
  --name "$(hostname)"
For Kubernetes clusters (on-premises, other clouds, or edge), the process is similar:

# Arc-enable an existing Kubernetes cluster
# Requires: kubectl context pointing at the cluster
az connectedk8s connect \
  --name "on-prem-k8s" \
  --resource-group hybrid-rg \
  --location eastus

# Enable GitOps (Flux) on the cluster — declarative config from Git
az k8s-configuration flux create \
  --resource-group hybrid-rg \
  --cluster-name on-prem-k8s \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --name prod-config \
  --url "https://github.com/myorg/gitops-repo" \
  --branch main \
  --kustomization name=base path="./clusters/on-prem"
Hybrid Connectivity: ExpressRoute and VPN Gateway

The network link between on-premises and Azure determines which workload patterns are feasible. Arc agents only need outbound HTTPS (control plane) — but database replication, storage sync, and burst compute require a reliable, low-latency data-plane path.
Option
Max Bandwidth
Typical RTT
SLA
Best For
ExpressRoute Standard
10 Gbps
< 5 ms (private circuit)
Prod databases, replication
ExpressRoute Ultra
100 Gbps
< 2 ms
HPC, large data migrations
Site-to-Site VPN (IKEv2)
10 Gbps (VpnGw5)
20–60 ms (internet)
Dev/test, backup sites, small offices
Azure Virtual WAN
Multiple circuits aggregated
Varies by circuit
Global enterprise with many branches

# Create an ExpressRoute circuit (shell side — provider provisions the physical link)
az network express-route create \
  --name "corp-to-azure" \
  --resource-group hybrid-rg \
  --location eastus \
  --bandwidth 1000 \
  --peering-location "Washington DC" \
  --provider "Equinix" \
  --sku-tier Standard \
  --sku-family MeteredData

# After provider provisions the link, enable private peering
az network express-route peering create \
  --circuit-name corp-to-azure \
  --resource-group hybrid-rg \
  --peering-type AzurePrivatePeering \
  --peer-asn 65100 \
  --primary-peer-address-prefix 192.168.1.0/30 \
  --secondary-peer-address-prefix 192.168.1.4/30 \
  --vlan-id 100
Hybrid Identity with Microsoft Entra Connect

Hybrid cloud demands hybrid identity: users must authenticate once and access both on-premises resources (file servers, internal apps) and Azure/SaaS services (M365, Azure portal, custom apps). Microsoft Entra Connect (formerly Azure AD Connect) is the synchronization engine that bridges on-premises Active Directory to Entra ID.
Password Hash Sync (PHS):
Hashed credential synced to cloud. Sign-in happens in Entra ID — works even if on-premises AD is unreachable. Recommended for most organizations. No ADFS required.
Pass-Through Authentication (PTA):
Cloud authentication request forwarded in real-time to on-premises AD via PTA agent. Password never leaves on-premises. Required when cloud-based hash sync violates policy.
Federation (ADFS):

Full SAML/WS-Fed token flow. Maximum customization (custom MFA, smart card auth, complex claim rules) but requires operating ADFS farm — high availability, patching, certificate rotation. Use only when PHS/PTA cannot satisfy requirements.
Hybrid Azure AD Join:
Domain-joined machines also register with Entra ID. Enables Conditional Access policies and SSO to cloud apps from corporate devices without requiring the user to re-authenticate.
Azure File Sync: Hybrid Storage

Azure File Sync enables a Windows Server to act as a local cache for Azure Files shares. Frequently accessed files stay on-premises for low-latency access; cold data is tiered to the cloud, freeing local disk. Multiple office locations can all sync to the same Azure Files share.

# Register a Windows Server with Azure File Sync service
Register-AzStorageSyncServer `
  -ResourceGroupName hybrid-rg `
  -StorageSyncServiceName corp-sync-service

# Create a sync group (ties Azure Files share to server endpoints)
New-AzStorageSyncGroup `
  -ResourceGroupName hybrid-rg `
  -StorageSyncServiceName corp-sync-service `
  -Name "finance-sync-group"

# Add cloud endpoint — points at an Azure Files share
New-AzStorageSyncCloudEndpoint `
  -ResourceGroupName hybrid-rg `
  -StorageSyncServiceName corp-sync-service `
  -SyncGroupName "finance-sync-group" `
  -StorageAccountResourceId "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/hybrid-rg/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/corpstorage" `
  -AzureFileShareName "finance"

# Add server endpoint — a local folder on the registered server
New-AzStorageSyncServerEndpoint `
  -ResourceGroupName hybrid-rg `
  -StorageSyncServiceName corp-sync-service `
  -SyncGroupName "finance-sync-group" `
  -ServerResourceId (Get-AzStorageSyncServer -ResourceGroupName hybrid-rg -StorageSyncServiceName corp-sync-service).ResourceId `
  -ServerLocalPath "D:\Finance" `
  -CloudTiering On `
  -VolumeFreeSpacePercent 20   # Keep 20% local disk free by tiering cold files
Azure Hybrid Benefit: License Portability

Azure Hybrid Benefit (AHB) lets organizations apply existing on-premises Windows Server and SQL Server licenses (with active Software Assurance) to Azure VMs and Azure SQL, substantially reducing compute costs.
Windows Server:

A 2-processor Standard license covers 2 Azure VMs up to 8 cores. Datacenter edition covers unlimited VMs. Savings: 40% off base Linux price (you pay only the infrastructure rate, not the Windows license surcharge).
SQL Server:

Each on-premises core maps to 1 Azure vCore in Azure SQL Database or Managed Instance. Enterprise Edition license → General Purpose or Business Critical. Savings: up to 55% vs pay-as-you-go SQL pricing.
Azure Arc SQL Server:
Arc-registered SQL Server instances on-premises also benefit — enables Azure Pay-as-you-go billing through Arc, activating extended security updates (ESU) for SQL 2012/2014 without migrating.
Common Hybrid Architecture Patterns
Pattern 1: Cloud Bursting

Run steady-state workloads on-premises (where CapEx is sunk) and automatically overflow peak load to Azure. The key requirement is that the public cloud deployment is pre-configured and idle, not provisioned on-demand — provisioning takes minutes, but burst traffic arrives in seconds.
Keep an Azure VMSS pre-scaled to minimum (1 instance) at all times so scale-out is fast.
Use Azure Traffic Manager or Application Gateway with a weighted routing rule: 100% on-prem normally, gradually shift to Azure as on-prem CPU exceeds threshold.
Ensure the app tier is stateless — session state in Redis or Azure Cache, files in Azure Files / Blob, DB connection via private endpoint.
Azure Stack HCI Supplemental Package supports native bursting from HCI clusters to Azure without re-architecting.
Pattern 2: Datacenter Extension

Treat Azure as a logical extension of the on-premises network. All Azure VNets are connected to on-premises via ExpressRoute; Azure VMs are assigned RFC 1918 addresses in the same IPAM plan as on-premises subnets. DNS resolves consistently across both environments via Azure Private DNS Resolver.

# Deploy Azure Private DNS Resolver for consistent hybrid DNS
az dns-resolver create \
  --name corp-dns-resolver \
  --resource-group hybrid-rg \
  --location eastus \
  --id "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/hybrid-rg/providers/Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/hub-vnet"

# Inbound endpoint (resolves Azure private DNS from on-prem)
az dns-resolver inbound-endpoint create \
  --dns-resolver-name corp-dns-resolver \
  --resource-group hybrid-rg \
  --name inbound-ep \
  --ip-configurations '[{"privateIpAllocationMethod":"Dynamic","id":"/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/hybrid-rg/providers/Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/hub-vnet/subnets/dns-inbound"}]'

# Outbound endpoint (forwards on-prem DNS queries from Azure)
az dns-resolver outbound-endpoint create \
  --dns-resolver-name corp-dns-resolver \
  --resource-group hybrid-rg \
  --name outbound-ep \
  --id "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/hybrid-rg/providers/Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/hub-vnet/subnets/dns-outbound"
Pattern 3: Edge-to-Cloud with Azure IoT
Industrial and retail scenarios generate data at the edge (factory floor, store POS, remote site) that needs local processing for latency reasons but centralized analytics in the cloud.
Azure IoT Edge:
Runs containerized Azure modules (Stream Analytics, ML models, custom logic) on edge devices. Manages offline operation — buffers events when disconnected, syncs to IoT Hub on reconnect.
Azure IoT Hub:
Cloud-side message broker receiving telemetry from thousands of edge devices. Routes to Stream Analytics, Event Hubs, or Azure Functions for processing and alerting.
Azure Stack Edge:
GPU-enabled hardware appliance running Azure services on-premises. Enables ML inference at the edge (e.g., visual quality inspection) with sub-millisecond latency, syncing results to Azure.
Hybrid DevOps and CI/CD
Hybrid cloud introduces the challenge of deploying to multiple targets (on-premises clusters and Azure services) from a single pipeline. Two approaches are common.
Azure DevOps with Self-Hosted Agents
Self-hosted agents run inside the corporate network, solving the challenge of deploying to on-premises targets that are not internet-accessible.

# azure-pipelines.yml — hybrid deployment
trigger:
  branches:
    include: [main]

stages:
- stage: DeployOnPrem
  jobs:
  - deployment: ToOnPremKubernetes
    pool:
      name: on-prem-agents    # self-hosted agent pool inside corporate network
    environment: on-prem-prod
    strategy:
      runOnce:
        deploy:
          steps:
          - task: KubernetesManifest@1
            inputs:
              action: deploy
              kubernetesServiceConnection: onprem-k8s-connection
              manifests: k8s/on-prem/*.yaml
              containers: $(containerRegistry)/myapp:$(Build.BuildId)

- stage: DeployAzure
  dependsOn: DeployOnPrem
  jobs:
  - deployment: ToAKS
    pool:
      vmImage: ubuntu-latest    # Microsoft-hosted agent for public cloud
    environment: azure-prod
    strategy:
      runOnce:
        deploy:
          steps:
          - task: AzureCLI@2
            inputs:
              azureSubscription: prod-service-connection
              scriptType: bash
              scriptLocation: inlineScript
              inlineScript: |
                az aks get-credentials -n prod-aks -g prod-rg
                kubectl apply -f k8s/azure/
                kubectl set image deployment/myapp myapp=$(containerRegistry)/myapp:$(Build.BuildId)
GitOps with Azure Arc and Flux

For Kubernetes-centric hybrid environments, GitOps via Arc eliminates the need for pipeline agents to reach into on-premises clusters. The cluster pulls its desired state from a Git repository — the pipeline only needs to commit to Git, not reach into protected networks.

# GitOps flow:
# 1. Developer pushes code → CI builds image → updates image tag in Git manifest
# 2. Flux (running in the cluster) detects the change and applies it
# 3. No pipeline agent needs network access to the cluster

# Check sync status
az k8s-configuration flux show \
  --resource-group hybrid-rg \
  --cluster-name on-prem-k8s \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --name prod-config \
  --query "{status:complianceState,lastApplied:statuses[0].appliedAt}"

# Force reconciliation (if Git updated but Flux hasn't synced yet)
az k8s-configuration flux update \
  --resource-group hybrid-rg \
  --cluster-name on-prem-k8s \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --name prod-config \
  --force-update true
Hybrid Cloud Challenges and Trade-offs
Hybrid cloud solves real problems but introduces complexity that pure public cloud avoids. Understanding these trade-offs prevents architecture regret.
Challenge
Root Cause
Mitigation
Increased network complexity
Two networks must be managed, secured, and monitored consistently
Hub-spoke topology, Azure Virtual WAN, centralized Network Watcher
Data gravity
Egress fees make moving large datasets between environments expensive
Process data close to where it lives; move only aggregates or results
Skills gap
Team must master both on-prem systems and Azure services
Arc unifies tooling (Azure CLI, Portal, Policy) — reduce unique tools per environment
Latency across the link
Even ExpressRoute adds 5–50 ms vs sub-ms local call
Design for async where possible; avoid chatty cross-boundary calls
Patch management sprawl
On-prem servers have separate patch cadence from PaaS services
Azure Update Manager via Arc — single view and orchestration for all servers
Security surface expansion
Each connectivity link is a potential attack vector
Defender for Cloud in hybrid mode, Zero Trust network policies, Conditional Access
Common Mistakes
Treating hybrid as a stepping stone, not a destination.
Some workloads (regulated data, latency-sensitive manufacturing) will stay on-premises permanently. Design the hybrid architecture for permanence, not just migration convenience.
Ignoring egress costs until the bill arrives.
Replicating terabytes of data daily from Azure to on-premises (for local analytics or compliance archiving) can cost more than the compute. Model data flows and egress fees at design time.
Inconsistent security policies.

On-premises assets managed with legacy Group Policy while Azure resources use modern Conditional Access creates gaps attackers exploit. Use Defender for Cloud in hybrid mode to enforce a baseline across both.
One ExpressRoute circuit with no redundancy.

ExpressRoute circuits are layer-3 connections that terminate at a meet-me location. Hardware failure at the edge router takes down all hybrid connectivity. Always provision two circuits via different providers or locations for 99.95%+ availability.
When to Choose Hybrid Cloud
Hybrid cloud is the right choice when at least one of these conditions is true:
A regulatory or contractual requirement mandates that specific data categories stay on-premises or within a geographic boundary that no available Azure region satisfies.
You have significant on-premises hardware investment (< 3 years into a 5-year refresh cycle) that cannot be written off — public cloud would require paying for both.
A subset of workloads requires deterministic sub-millisecond latency (OT networks, real-time control systems) that network round-trips to Azure cannot provide.
You are mid-migration: legacy systems cannot move yet but new cloud-native services must integrate with them now.
Seasonal or batch workloads justify cloud burst economics without permanently migrating the steady-state tier.
Hybrid Cloud Cost Modeling

Hybrid cloud cost accounting is harder than pure cloud because costs span two operating models: the capital-expense model of on-premises and the operational-expense model of Azure. Without explicit modeling, hybrid often appears cheaper than it is because the sunk cost of on-premises hardware is not compared fairly against the full TCO of cloud-only.
Full-Cost Visibility with Azure Cost Management
Azure Cost Management + Billing supports hybrid cost tracking via two mechanisms:
Arc resource cost:

Arc-enabled SQL Server, Kubernetes, and Stack HCI resources appear in Cost Management with their Azure service charges (Update Manager, Defender for Cloud, Extended Security Updates) alongside public cloud resources. You can tag and group both in the same budget view.
Azure Migrate assessments:

Azure Migrate estimates the Azure cost of migrating on-premises VMs (as Azure VMs, Azure SQL, AKS) and compares it against current on-premises cost (hardware amortization + power + cooling + staff). Use this to decide which workloads are cheaper in Azure.
Cost Category
On-Premises
Azure Public Cloud
Hybrid
Compute
CapEx amortized over 3–5 yr
PAYG / Reserved (1- or 3-yr)
CapEx on-prem + PAYG Azure burst
Network
ISP bandwidth, DC cross-connects
Egress $0.087/GB, VNet peering
ExpressRoute port fee + egress to internet
Management overhead
High (patching, hardware faults)
Low (managed services)
Medium (Arc reduces on-prem overhead)
Licenses
SA + perpetual, own the license
Included in Azure price (or BYOL)
Hybrid Benefit: use SA licenses in Azure
ExpressRoute Cost Example

An ExpressRoute 1 Gbps circuit in the US East region (Standard, Metered) costs approximately $55/month for the port fee plus $0.025/GB outbound data from Azure. A 10 TB/month replication workload would add $250/month in data charges. Compare this against VPN Gateway SKU VpnGw3 at ~$560/month with internet egress at $0.087/GB — the math favors ExpressRoute above ~3 TB/month of outbound data.
Security Operations Across Hybrid Environments

Hybrid cloud expands the attack surface. A security breach on-premises can pivot to Azure via the ExpressRoute link, and vice versa. Security operations must span both environments with a unified view.
Microsoft Defender for Cloud (Hybrid Mode)

When Arc agents are installed on on-premises servers, Defender for Cloud extends its Secure Score, vulnerability assessments, and threat detection to those machines. A single dashboard shows the combined security posture.

# Enable Defender for Servers on Arc-enabled machines
az security pricing create \
  --name VirtualMachines \
  --tier Standard

# Defender auto-deploys the MDE (Microsoft Defender for Endpoint) extension
# to all Arc-registered servers in the subscription.
# View alerts across hybrid environment:
az security alert list \
  --query "[].{name:name,state:properties.state,severity:properties.severity,resource:properties.resourceIdentifiers[0].azureResourceId}" \
  -o table
Microsoft Sentinel for Hybrid SIEM

Microsoft Sentinel (cloud-native SIEM) ingests logs from both Azure resources and on-premises systems via data connectors. Log Analytics agents (or AMA) on Arc-enabled servers forward Windows Event logs, Syslog, and CEF-formatted security events to the same Sentinel workspace used for cloud resources.

# Connect on-premises Windows servers to Sentinel via AMA (Azure Monitor Agent)
# First, create a Data Collection Rule targeting the Log Analytics workspace
az monitor data-collection rule create \
  --name dcr-windows-events \
  --resource-group hybrid-rg \
  --location eastus \
  --data-flows '[{"streams":["Microsoft-SecurityEvent"],"destinations":["sentinel-workspace"]}]' \
  --destinations '{"logAnalytics":[{"name":"sentinel-workspace","workspaceResourceId":"/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/sentinel-rg/providers/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/sentinel-ws"}]}'

# Associate the DCR with Arc-enabled servers
az monitor data-collection rule association create \
  --name dcr-assoc-onprem \
  --resource-group hybrid-rg \
  --rule-id "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/hybrid-rg/providers/Microsoft.Insights/dataCollectionRules/dcr-windows-events" \
  --resource "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/hybrid-rg/providers/Microsoft.HybridCompute/machines/onprem-server-01"
Zero Trust for Hybrid Environments

Hybrid cloud is where Zero Trust principles matter most, because the network boundary no longer cleanly separates trusted (on-prem) from untrusted (internet). Once ExpressRoute is in place, an attacker who compromises an on-premises server has a low-latency path into the Azure VNet.
Verify explicitly:
All access (on-prem to Azure, Azure to on-prem) goes through Entra ID Conditional Access — check device compliance and MFA even for internal requests.
Least privilege:
Azure RBAC and AD group membership are synchronized via Arc and Entra Connect — no separate on-premises permission grant needed for Arc resources.
Assume breach:
Microsegment the ExpressRoute-connected Azure subnet. On-premises machines should only reach their specific Azure Private Endpoints, not the entire VNet.
Unified Monitoring for Hybrid Cloud

Observability across hybrid environments requires a single pane of glass. Azure Monitor, backed by a Log Analytics workspace, serves as the hub — Azure resources, Arc-enabled servers, and Kubernetes clusters all forward metrics and logs to the same workspace.

# KQL query: CPU health across all servers (Azure VMs + Arc on-prem servers)
# Run in Log Analytics workspace linked to Azure Monitor
# Perf table includes data from both Azure and Arc-enabled machines

// Top 10 most CPU-loaded servers (last 24h) — hybrid fleet
Perf
| where TimeGenerated > ago(24h)
| where ObjectName == "Processor Information" and CounterName == "% Processor Time"
| summarize AvgCPU = avg(CounterValue) by Computer, _ResourceId
| top 10 by AvgCPU desc
| extend IsAzureVM = _ResourceId contains "/virtualMachines/"
| extend IsArcServer = _ResourceId contains "/machines/"
| project Computer, AvgCPU = round(AvgCPU, 1), Type = iff(IsAzureVM, "Azure VM", iff(IsArcServer, "Arc Server", "Unknown"))

Azure Monitor Workbooks provide pre-built dashboards for hybrid scenarios. The "Servers" workbook under Arc shows availability, CPU, memory, disk, and network for all Arc-registered machines alongside Azure VMs in a single view.
Summary
Hybrid cloud is a permanent, integrated operating model — not a temporary state between on-premises and cloud-only.
Azure Arc is the cornerstone of Azure hybrid management: it projects on-premises and multi-cloud resources into ARM, enabling unified Policy, RBAC, Defender, and Update Manager.
ExpressRoute provides dedicated private connectivity; VPN Gateway provides encrypted internet-based connectivity — choose based on bandwidth, latency SLA, and cost requirements.
Microsoft Entra Connect (PHS or PTA) unifies identity so on-premises users get SSO to Azure and SaaS services without a separate cloud account.
Common hybrid patterns: cloud bursting, datacenter extension, and edge-to-cloud IoT each solve different portions of the constraint set.
Key risks: egress costs, security policy gaps between environments, and single-circuit ExpressRoute failure — mitigate each at design time.
